_schema.description: Safety flags for high-risk activities. Used by pipeline/synthesizer.py to inject required disclaimers. Language sourced from REI expert advice articles — see source_urls per entry.

risk_levels.critical: Life-threatening if improperly equipped or untrained. Hard gate: synthesizer MUST include disclaimer and gear requirements.
risk_levels.high: Significant injury risk. Synthesizer MUST include disclaimer.
risk_levels.moderate: Meaningful risk with known mitigations. Synthesizer SHOULD include safety note.

fields.primary_disclaimer: The exact text that must appear in the recommendation response.
fields.training_requirement: Required instruction or certification statement. Include when activity requires professional training.
fields.required_gear_statements: Gear items that MUST be mentioned if recommending products for this activity.
fields.additional_warnings: Supporting safety points drawn from REI language.
fields.certifying_bodies: Professional organizations REI references for this activity.
fields.source_urls: REI pages where this language was sourced.

mountaineering.primary_disclaimer: Climbing safety is your responsibility. No article or video can replace qualified instruction and experience.
mountaineering.training_requirement: REI recommends signing up for classes taught by trained professionals. Consider hiring a guide recognized by the American Mountain Guides Association (AMGA) if you are new to mountaineering.
required_gear_statements: Ice axe and self-arrest skills are required before traveling on snow slopes or glaciers.
Crevasse rescue skills are required for glacier travel.
Crampons rated C2 or higher are required for technical terrain.
additional_warnings: Always leave a trip itinerary with someone at home, including your route, campsite locations, and expected return time.
Train for a minimum of 16 weeks before your trip depending on your fitness level.
Before you attempt your own climb, you first need to master technical skills and acquire a sense for how to travel in the mountains.

alpine_climbing.primary_disclaimer: Climbing safety is your responsibility. No article or video can replace qualified instruction and experience.
alpine_climbing.training_requirement: REI recommends professional instruction before attempting alpine routes. Find a guide recognized by the American Mountain Guides Association (AMGA).
required_gear_statements: Ice axe, crampons, and self-arrest skills are required.
Glacier travel requires roped-team skills and crevasse rescue training.
Helmet is required.
additional_warnings: Always leave a trip itinerary with someone at home.
Check weather and route conditions before your climb.

ice_climbing.primary_disclaimer: Climbing safety is your responsibility. No article or video can replace qualified instruction and experience.
ice_climbing.training_requirement: The first several times you go ice climbing should be on top rope with an experienced guide or mentor — not just a fellow novice. REI recommends finding qualified instruction before leading ice.
required_gear_statements: Technical crampons (C3, step-in binding) are required — general hiking crampons are not sufficient.
Two technical ice axes are required for vertical ice.
Helmet with minimal ventilation is required — you will be exposed to falling ice debris.
Harness, rope, and belay device are required.
additional_warnings: Expect significant falling debris — choose a helmet designed specifically for this exposure.
Ice conditions change rapidly with temperature — check conditions before each session.

rock_climbing.primary_disclaimer: Safety is your responsibility. No internet article or video can replace proper instruction and experience.
rock_climbing.training_requirement: Belaying is a foundational skill that requires instruction from a qualified teacher. REI recommends taking a top-rope climbing course before climbing outdoors.
required_gear_statements: Helmet is mandatory when climbing outdoors.
Harness and belay device are required.
Belay certification is required to belay a partner.
additional_warnings: Always use standard climbing commands — deviation is a safety risk, not a preference.
Indoor proficiency should be established before moving outdoors.
Lead climbing requires additional instruction beyond basic top-rope skills.

ski_touring.primary_disclaimer: If you want to ski or snowboard in the backcountry, it's crucial that you enroll in avalanche safety training taught by a qualified professional.
ski_touring.training_requirement: REI recommends at minimum a Level 1 avalanche course certified by the American Institute for Avalanche Research and Education (AIARE) before heading into the backcountry. Take a free avalanche awareness class first, then pursue formal training.
required_gear_statements: The Big Three are mandatory for every member of your group: avalanche transceiver (beacon), snow probe, and avalanche shovel.
Carrying avalanche safety gear is useless without the training to use it.
All members of your group must carry essential avalanche rescue gear and know how to use it.
additional_warnings: Check the avalanche forecast before every outing — regional backcountry avalanche centers publish daily forecasts.
Ski one at a time through exposed terrain so someone always has eyes on the group.
Red flags requiring immediate reassessment: cracking, whoomping sounds, recent avalanche activity, wind loading, and warming temperatures.
Most avalanche activity starts on slopes between 30 and 45 degrees. Carry an inclinometer.
Do not blindly follow existing skin tracks without understanding the terrain and hazards.
Your partner's best chance of survival is rescue within 10 minutes.
certifying_bodies: American Institute for Avalanche Research and Education (AIARE)

snowboarding_backcountry.primary_disclaimer: It's essential to have proper training and equipment to travel safely if you leave the boundaries of a ski area. Avalanches are a real concern in off-piste terrain.
snowboarding_backcountry.training_requirement: REI recommends at minimum a Level 1 avalanche course certified by AIARE before heading into the backcountry.
required_gear_statements: The must-have gear for every backcountry explorer: avalanche transceiver, snow shovel, and probe. Knowing how to use this gear is equally as important as having it.
Helmet is required.
additional_warnings: Check the avalanche forecast before every backcountry session.
Consider your resort-level ability before transitioning to backcountry terrain.
Your partner's best chance of survival is rescue within 10 minutes.
certifying_bodies: American Institute for Avalanche Research and Education (AIARE)

avalanche_safety.primary_disclaimer: Remember: Safety is your responsibility. Carrying avalanche safety gear is useless without the training to use it.
avalanche_safety.training_requirement: You need to train, practice, and refresh training each year. REI recommends AIARE Level 1 certification for anyone traveling in avalanche terrain.
required_gear_statements: The Big Three are non-negotiable: avalanche transceiver, snow probe, and avalanche shovel.
Every member of your group must carry all three items and know how to use them.
additional_warnings: Practice rescue skills every chance you get.
Your partner's best chance of survival is rescue within 10 minutes — statistically this is the window before survival rates drop sharply.
After an avalanche: stop and assess further risk, count heads, note the last-seen location of each missing person, call for help immediately.
certifying_bodies: American Institute for Avalanche Research and Education (AIARE)

whitewater_kayaking.primary_disclaimer: Always wear a personal flotation device (PFD) and never take it off while on the water. Dress for the water temperature, not the air temperature.
whitewater_kayaking.training_requirement: For sit-in kayaks, everyone in the group must know how to do a wet exit and at least one person must know how to perform a self-rescue and T-rescue. Take safety courses before trying Class III or above rapids.
required_gear_statements: A properly secured PFD is mandatory — do not step into the boat without it.
Helmet is required for Class III rapids and above.
Wetsuit or drysuit is required when water temperature is below 60°F.
Cold-water immersion effects can occur in water as warm as 70°F — dress accordingly.
additional_warnings: Never paddle alone — always go with another paddler who can summon help.
Keep your group within earshot or a whistle blast of one another at all times.
Check river gauges and weather forecasts before every trip. Scout unfamiliar rapids before running them.
Risks of cold-water capsizing include immediate lung and heart shock, drowning, and hypothermia.
Leave a float plan with someone on shore including group names, contact info, and expected return time.

winter_camping.primary_disclaimer: Frostbite and hypothermia are legitimate concerns while winter camping. You need to actively take care of yourself and your hiking partners.
winter_camping.training_requirement: Learn to recognize the early signs of frostbite and hypothermia before your trip. Consider a wilderness first aid course for remote winter travel.
required_gear_statements: A 4-season tent rated for winter conditions is required.
A sleeping bag rated at least 10°F below the expected overnight low is required — beginners should add additional buffer.
Two sleeping pads are recommended — insulation from the ground is critical.
A stove rated for cold temperatures is required.
additional_warnings: If you feel your fingers or toes getting cold, stop immediately and warm them — do not push on.
Seek medical treatment immediately if frostbite results in blistering.
Moderate and severe hypothermia require immediate evacuation.
Choose a campsite sheltered from wind and free of avalanche danger. Pack down the snow before pitching your tent.
Always travel with a partner in remote winter conditions.

snowshoeing_avalanche_terrain.primary_disclaimer: If you are snowshoeing in avalanche terrain, the same rules apply as backcountry skiing: carry avalanche safety gear and know how to use it.
snowshoeing_avalanche_terrain.training_requirement: Take an avalanche awareness course before traveling in avalanche terrain on snowshoes.
required_gear_statements: Avalanche transceiver, probe, and shovel are required in avalanche terrain.
All group members must carry and know how to use the Big Three.
additional_warnings: Check the avalanche forecast before heading out.
Stay off steep slopes between 30–45 degrees.
certifying_bodies: American Institute for Avalanche Research and Education (AIARE)
